askNext: What exactly triggers fallback?
Does the fallback support the same tools and schemas?
Do dashboards separate primary and fallback quality?
fallback-model.body: Fallback models are insurance. Insurance should not be a surprise to the customer.
fallback-model.example: "The fallback model kept the site up and the legal answers creative."
fallback-model.misuse: Configured silently so users never know quality changed, or pointed at a model that cannot perform the same tools and formats.
fallback-model.practicalMeaning: Define trigger conditions, quality expectations, feature parity, and user-visible degradation signals. Test fallbacks as first-class paths.

translations.label: technical meaning
translations.text: A fallback model is a secondary model or endpoint used when the primary model fails, times out, is rate-limited, or is policy-blocked, preserving availability at possible quality cost.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Define trigger conditions, quality expectations, feature parity, and user-visible degradation signals.

fallback-model.warningLabel: Availability without honesty is a soft outage.
fallback-model.whyExists: Single-model dependence is fragile. Production systems need degraded modes that still answer.

askNext: What exact meaning of Fallback policy is being used here?
What changes operationally if this label is removed from the sentence?
Is this naming a mechanism, a vendor choice, a risk, or a wish?
fallback-policy.body: Fallback policy belongs here because AI systems become organisational systems as soon as people rely on them. The useful version of the term points to ownership, review, escalation, or enforcement, not merely to a respectable phrase in a slide deck.
fallback-policy.example: "The fallback policy discussion began as process paperwork and ended as the only reason the agent was allowed anywhere near production."
fallback-policy.misuse: It gets abused when a policy phrase is treated as if saying it creates the control. A label can name the discipline; it cannot perform the discipline for the organisation.
fallback-policy.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means writing down who can act, who must approve, what gets logged, what can be reversed, and which failures become incidents rather than interesting anecdotes.

translations.label: operator meaning
translations.text: The part where permission, accountability, and review have to become real.

fallback-policy.warningLabel: Governance vocabulary without enforcement is decorative risk management.
fallback-policy.whyExists: It exists because the problems around Model Router are not only model problems. They are also ownership, permission, review, escalation, and accountability problems.

askNext: What exact meaning of Fallback routing is being used here?
What changes operationally if this label is removed from the sentence?
Is this naming a mechanism, a vendor choice, a risk, or a wish?
fallback-routing.body: Fallback routing belongs in the dictionary because implementation detail changes the product conversation. The useful question is not whether the term sounds modern, but what constraint, cost, interface, or failure mode it names.
fallback-routing.example: "The team said fallback routing as though it closed the matter; the postmortem revealed it was where the matter had been hiding."
fallback-routing.misuse: It gets abused when the label is treated as a solution rather than a place where engineering still has to make choices, measure trade-offs, and own the consequences.
fallback-routing.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means checking the boundary conditions: inputs, outputs, latency, permissions, reliability, cost, observability, and whether the chosen abstraction survives real use.

translations.label: engineer meaning
translations.text: A concrete implementation concern wearing a compact label.

fallback-routing.warningLabel: Technical vocabulary is not implementation evidence.
fallback-routing.whyExists: It exists because conversations around OpenRouter need sharper language than "the AI bit" when the real issue is interfaces, constraints, failure modes, cost, or system behaviour.

askNext: Is the Figma file current enough to guide implementation?
Which design tokens, components, and states are actually represented?
Does the agent receive structured design context, or just a screenshot with confidence?
figma.body: Figma's AI-era importance is not just that designs can become code. It is that design context can become part of the agent's working memory before the first component is written.
figma.example: "The agent implemented the Figma screen accurately, including the part where nobody had designed the error state."
figma.misuse: It gets treated as if a neat Figma file guarantees a neat product. Dev Mode, component metadata, and MCP context can improve implementation, but they do not settle product ambiguity, missing states, accessibility, or the difference between a component library and a living app.
figma.practicalMeaning: In practice Figma means design-system source material, inspectable component context, comments, prototypes, and a growing bridge into coding agents that can read design intent before writing UI.

translations.label: designer meaning
translations.text: The shared design surface where intent, comments, components, and unresolved arguments gather.

translations.label: engineer meaning
translations.text: Useful structured context, as long as the design file has not become a historical document.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A strong handoff and collaboration platform that agents can now query more directly, not a guarantee that the app will make sense.

figma.warningLabel: Design context helps agents. It does not absolve anyone from checking the interface.
figma.whyExists: It exists because teams need a shared place to design, discuss, inspect, and hand off product interfaces without passing static files around like office furniture.

askNext: What is the root the agent can see?
Are secrets mounted into that tree?
How are writes reviewed before merge?
filesystem-scope.body: Filesystem scope is property law for agents. Survey the boundaries.
filesystem-scope.example: "Filesystem scope kept the agent inside the repo after it tried to 'clean up' SSH keys."
filesystem-scope.misuse: Set to the user's home directory for convenience. Convenience is how secrets and unrelated repos get rewritten.
filesystem-scope.practicalMeaning: Default to a task worktree, deny dotfile and secret paths unless required, and log file mutations. Prefer ephemeral copies for experiments.

translations.label: technical meaning
translations.text: Filesystem scope is the policy defining which directories and files an agent may read or write during a task, often enforced via sandboxes, worktrees, or OS permissions.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Default to a task worktree, deny dotfile and secret paths unless required, and log file mutations.

filesystem-scope.warningLabel: Broad file access is root-adjacent social engineering waiting to happen.
filesystem-scope.whyExists: File access is where agents cause durable damage. Scope turns ambient computer access into a defined workspace.

askNext: What behaviour are we trying to change that prompting cannot hold steady?
What data will teach that behaviour without teaching unwanted shortcuts?
How will we know the tuned model is better, not just narrower?
fine-tuning.body: Good fine-tuning is usually boring in the best way. It removes repeated prompt hacks and makes behaviour steadier. Bad fine-tuning makes a model narrower, more brittle, and harder to reason about.
fine-tuning.example: "They did not fine-tune because the team loved custom models; they did it because the required output format had to be boringly consistent."
fine-tuning.misuse: It is often sold as the universal answer whenever prompts disappoint. In reality, many problems blamed on a lack of fine-tuning are retrieval problems, product design problems, or evaluation problems. Fine-tuning can help, but it can also overfit and create fresh failure modes.
fine-tuning.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means curating a task-specific dataset, deciding how much of the base model to adapt, training on that smaller dataset, and checking whether the resulting behaviour improved enough to justify the effort and operational complexity.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A targeted attempt to move model behaviour with additional training rather than endless prompt cajoling.

fine-tuning.whyExists: General-purpose models are broad, not bespoke. Fine-tuning exists for cases where prompting alone is too unstable, too expensive, or too weak to reliably shape behaviour.

askNext: Which model builds are pinned versus floating tags?
What are rate limits and region options?
How do you fail over without rewriting clients?
fireworks-ai.body: Fireworks AI is a place tokens go to become bills. Measure before marrying.
fireworks-ai.example: "They moved inference to Fireworks AI after the overnight batch made the laptop cry."
fireworks-ai.misuse: Assumed interchangeable with every other provider. Throughput, features, and model support differ enough to break naive multi-cloud fantasies.
fireworks-ai.practicalMeaning: Benchmark your models and prompt shapes; check fine-tuning, LoRA, and compound-AI features only if you will use them.

translations.label: product meaning
translations.text: Fireworks AI is an inference and model-serving platform focused on fast deployment of open and custom models via API and infrastructure tooling.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Benchmark your models and prompt shapes; check fine-tuning, LoRA, and compound-AI features only if you will use them.

fireworks-ai.warningLabel: Host branding is not a latency SLA.
fireworks-ai.whyExists: Developers wanted low-latency open-model endpoints without building a full serving stack. Vendors filled that gap.

askNext: Which FlashAttention version does your stack pin?
Do training and inference both use it where expected?
What is the measured speedup on your shapes?
flash-attention.body: FlashAttention is plumbing that changed the possible. Plumbing still has versions.
flash-attention.example: "FlashAttention cut memory enough to raise batch size without rewriting the model."
flash-attention.misuse: Cited as a product feature users configure. It is infrastructure; correctness and version compatibility matter more than the brand name in slides.
flash-attention.practicalMeaning: Use maintained kernels via your framework, confirm numerical settings, and re-benchmark when CUDA/framework versions change.

translations.label: technical meaning
translations.text: FlashAttention is a family of exact attention algorithms and kernels that reduce high-bandwidth memory traffic via tiling and recomputation, enabling faster and more memory-efficient attention on GPUs

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Use maintained kernels via your framework, confirm numerical settings, and re-benchmark when CUDA/framework versions change.

flash-attention.warningLabel: Kernel choice is part of your reproducibility story.
flash-attention.whyExists: Standard attention materialises large matrices that crush memory bandwidth. Better kernels unlocked practical longer contexts and larger batches.

askNext: What exact meaning of Fluid Compute is being used here?
What changes operationally if this label is removed from the sentence?
Is this naming a mechanism, a vendor choice, a risk, or a wish?
fluid-compute.body: Fluid Compute earns its own page because branded nouns often hide decisions about platform control, pricing, permissions, and operational dependency. This entry keeps the name visible without letting it do the architecture's work.
fluid-compute.example: "Fluid Compute came up in the review, which was useful only after the team clarified whether they meant the product, the platform, the model family, or the vendor relationship."
fluid-compute.misuse: It gets abused when the name is used as a substitute for specifying the exact product surface, model, API, entitlement, pricing constraint, or operational dependency under discussion.
fluid-compute.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means someone should ask what is being bought, integrated, routed through, depended on, or compared before the logo is allowed to stand in for the architecture.

translations.label: procurement meaning
translations.text: Treat the name as a dependency to inspect, not a badge to admire.

fluid-compute.warningLabel: A brand-shaped noun is not a technical specification.
fluid-compute.whyExists: The term matters because discussions around Vercel often need to separate the actual technical choice from the brand, distribution channel, contract, or ecosystem wrapped around it.

askNext: Is the FDE creating reusable product learning or permanent custom glue?
What operational knowledge comes back from the field?
Does this role scale the product, or hide its unfinished parts?
forward-deployed-engineer.body: The best FDEs make the product better. The worst cases make the product optional.
forward-deployed-engineer.example: "The FDE made the pilot work by wiring the model into three legacy systems, two spreadsheets, and one undocumented process everyone described as temporary."
forward-deployed-engineer.misuse: The role gets romanticised as heroic execution while sometimes masking a product that needs too much bespoke labour to work repeatably.
forward-deployed-engineer.practicalMeaning: In practice it means customer proximity, integration work, rapid prototyping, domain learning, support escalation, and a constant tension between scalable product and customised delivery.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A field-embedded engineer turning customer mess into working software, product feedback, or bespoke dependency.

forward-deployed-engineer.whyExists: Complex AI and enterprise systems often fail in the gap between product promises and customer reality. FDEs sit near that gap, translating messy workflows into working implementations and product feedback.

askNext: Which FP8 variant and scaling scheme?
Are critical layers kept in higher precision?
Do evals match FP16/BF16 baselines closely enough?
fp8.body: FP8 is performance with fine print. Read the fine print before the outage.
fp8.example: "FP8 training looked fast until the scaler and the checkpoint format disagreed."
fp8.misuse: Treated as a single standard. Format details, scaling, and kernel support differ and can silently break numerics.
fp8.practicalMeaning: Pin format and library versions, validate loss curves and evals, and confirm accelerators actually run FP8 kernels for your ops.

translations.label: technical meaning
translations.text: FP8 is a family of 8-bit floating-point formats used to reduce memory and increase throughput in modern training and inference stacks, with multiple variants and scaling strategies.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Pin format and library versions, validate loss curves and evals, and confirm accelerators actually run FP8 kernels for your ops.

fp8.warningLabel: Bit width is not a complete numerical recipe.
fp8.whyExists: Hardware vendors added FP8 paths as models grew. Software stacks followed to exploit the performance.

askNext: Frontier by which metric: benchmark spread, multimodal breadth, tool use, or cost-to-quality ratio?
Does the use case actually need a top-tier model, or just a reliable one?
What trade-offs come with using the most capable model available?
frontier-model.body: The term becomes most useful when followed immediately by the phrase "for what task?" Otherwise it tends to drift into status perfume.
frontier-model.example: "The company pitched a frontier-model strategy. Operations translated that into a calmer sentence about high cost, external dependency, and unclear product fit."
frontier-model.misuse: It is often treated as a formal badge rather than a loose ranking label. Vendors, commentators, and investors may use it to imply inevitability, superiority, or strategic importance beyond what the actual use case warrants.
frontier-model.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means a large, expensive model family with broad capability, strong benchmarks, and significant operational or policy attention. For many products, the question is not whether a model is frontier. The question is whether it is necessary.

translations.label: investor meaning
translations.text: This company is close enough to the top of the capability race to sound strategically important.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A high-end model near the current performance frontier, whether or not your workload needs that much firepower.

frontier-model.warningLabel: Useful shorthand, not a formal category. Often as much status signal as technical description.
frontier-model.whyExists: The term exists because the field needed a way to talk about the leading edge of capability without listing every model individually. It can be useful shorthand when discussing high-end systems, their risks, and their costs.

askNext: Which task category, harness, model, and trial count produced this score?
What correctness gate must pass before performance improvements count?
Did extra runtime improve the solution, recover from failure, or only delay submission?
frontierswe.body: No built-in diagram is attached because the useful mental model is a time-and-quality evaluation surface across heterogeneous technical tasks, which the current catalogue does not depict.
frontierswe.example: "The agent spent eight hours on FrontierSWE and submitted the approach it had discovered in minute eleven, now supported by considerably more evidence of confidence."
frontierswe.misuse: A leaderboard rank is treated as a pure model property even though the harness, tool environment, time limit, cost, stopping behaviour, repeated trials, and task-specific verifier all shape the outcome. Spending longer can reveal persistence or merely extend the mistake.
frontierswe.practicalMeaning: Compare results only when the model, harness, task version, tool access, time and token budgets, trial count, correctness gates, and performance scoring match. Inspect trajectories for premature submission, reward hacking, invalid shortcuts, and useful recovery after failed experiments.

translations.label: evaluation meaning
translations.text: Give coding agents genuinely difficult multi-hour technical work and score the verified result, not the length of the attempt.

frontierswe.warningLabel: Model, harness, budget, and verifier jointly produce the rank.
frontierswe.whyExists: Short issue-resolution benchmarks do not measure whether an agent can research an unfamiliar technical domain, optimise systems, run experiments, recover from failed approaches, and validate a result over several hours. FrontierSWE makes those behaviours central.

askNext: Who validates the arguments before execution?
What tools is the model allowed to call, and under what conditions?
How are failures, partial successes, and risky actions surfaced?
function-calling.body: Function calling is one of the clearest places where AI product language can mislead. The impressive part is not merely that the model chose a tool. The important part is whether the surrounding system kept that choice legible and safe.
function-calling.example: "The assistant did not refund the customer by itself; it produced a structured request, the service checked policy, and only then did the refund tool run."
function-calling.misuse: It gets described as if the model itself is performing the external action. Usually it is not. The model proposes the call. Your software validates it, executes it, and handles the consequences. If that surrounding code is sloppy, the elegance of the schema will not save you.
function-calling.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means JSON schemas, argument validation, permission controls, retries, and post-tool reasoning. Useful systems treat function calling as structured intent, not an excuse to trust a model near production infrastructure.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The model emits a structured request; the real system still has to decide whether to trust, reject, or execute it.

function-calling.whyExists: Models are good at interpreting language but bad at directly mutating real systems without control. Function calling exists to separate decision-making about which action to take from the actual execution of that action.
